title: Choosing paper for bookbinding
subtitle: Weight, grain direction, and format for text blocks and covers
excerpt: Paper weight, grain direction, and surface all affect how a binding holds, opens, and wears. The right paper for a Coptic-stitch notebook is not the right paper for a marbled endpaper or a paste-paper cover. This article walks through the decisions a binder makes when specifying paper.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on paper and materials. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock. Supplementary reference: Cyril Davenport, The Book: Its History and Development (1907), chapter on paper manufacture and selection.

content.text: Every bookbinding decision flows through three paper properties: weight in
gsm
grain direction

, and surface texture. Get all three right and the book opens smoothly, folds cleanly, and wears well. Get grain direction wrong and the covers warp, the signatures buckle, and the pages fight the reader every time they turn.
Weight

Text-block paper for multi-signature sewn bindings (Coptic, long-stitch, French-link) works best at 80 to 100 gsm. Lighter than 70 gsm and the signatures become fragile at the fold and the ink bleeds on most surfaces. Heavier than 120 gsm and the text block becomes too stiff to sew cleanly, and the signatures resist the gentle curvature that a rounded spine requires.
Pamphlet-stitch bindings (single signature) tolerate heavier paper because the single-fold does not need to flex repeatedly. A pamphlet notebook in 130 gsm cartridge is sturdy and holds ink well.

Endpapers run 100 to 140 gsm: heavy enough to carry the hinge stress at the spine, light enough to fold flat. Marbled-paper endpapers work well in the 90 to 120 gsm range, the mordanting adds a little rigidity that partially compensates for lighter base sheets.
Grain direction

Grain direction is the most critical property for binding. Paper expands and contracts across the grain as humidity changes. If the grain runs across the spine (against the fold), the text block will curl and the covers will warp inward or outward with seasonal humidity.

For an A5 book bound on the long edge (spine at the left), each signature folds an A4 sheet in half: the folded dimension is 210 mm (the short edge of A4). The grain must run parallel to this 210 mm fold, that is, parallel to the long dimension of the A4 sheet. This is
long-grain

Testing grain direction: bend a sheet loosely, without creasing, in one direction, then the other. The bend with the grain feels easy and springs back cleanly. The bend against the grain feels slightly stiffer and the paper may show very fine waviness along the fold.

attrs.body: Specialist paper suppliers mark grain direction on the label: 'Long grain' or 'Short grain', or using the notation A4-L (long grain) or A4-S (short grain). Ream packs from office-supply retailers rarely mark grain direction, but most A4 office paper is long-grain by default. Check with the supplier if ordering for a binding project.
attrs.title: Paper suppliers and grain marking

content.text: Surface

Surface texture determines how the paper takes different media. Smooth cartridge (90 to 130 gsm) is the all-purpose text-block surface: it takes pencil, ballpoint, fineliner, and most fountain pens without bleed-through. Hot-press watercolour paper is the choice for calligraphy with watercolour or gouache, the smooth sizing holds detail. Cold-press watercolour paper (textured surface) works for wash and sketching but causes most dip pens to catch on the tooth.

Ink bleed-through (show-through) depends on the paper's opacity and how heavily sized it is. A thin, lightly sized paper will shadow-print the reverse side under heavy ink coverage. For books that will be written in with fountain pens or dip pens, choose a well-sized paper, Cockerell noted that a good bond paper 'resists the pen and allows a clean stroke on the surface without sinking'.
Worked examples

A five-signature Coptic-stitch A5 notebook in 90 gsm long-grain cartridge has forty pages. The text block is light and flexible. The spine curves naturally under the Coptic chain. The same book in 130 gsm
short-grain
cartridge will be stiff. The signatures will not fold flat. The covers will bow within a few weeks.

A Japanese stab-bound booklet uses the paper differently. The sheets are not folded. They sit flat. The awl pierces through all layers at once. Grain direction still matters for the cover paper. The cover will warp if the grain runs across the spine edge. The text-block sheets can be heavier than for a sewn book. 100 to 120 gsm works well for stab binding, where 90 gsm is the better choice for a folded-signature book.
Cover boards and cover paper

Bookbinding board, grey board, comes in 1.5 mm, 2 mm, and 3 mm thicknesses. For A5 and A6 formats, 2 mm is the standard: stiff enough to protect the text block without being so heavy the book is hard to hold open. For A4 and larger formats, 3 mm board keeps the covers from flexing under the weight of the text block.

Grey board has a grain direction too, and it must match the text block. Boards cut against the grain will cup as soon as the cover paper is glued to the outside face. Order boards labelled with grain direction, or test the same way as paper: flex gently in each direction; the easier bend is with the grain.
